risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Two randomisation lists are computer generated by a statistician otherwise not involved in the study." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence prepared by an independent statistician, which is a truly random method and is sufficient for Low risk.

Quote: "Two randomisation lists are computer generated by a statistician otherwise not involved in the study. The lists are stored by the two regional study coordinators. After the definitive inclusion of a general practice into the study, the coordinator of the respective region calls the coordinator of the other region who, after registering the practice, hands out the allocation information." Comment: Allocation was concealed from the recruiter because the assignment lists were held by regional coordinators and the enrolling coordinator had to contact the other region's coordinator to receive the allocation only after definitive inclusion of the practice, indicating no foreknowledge of the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The intervention was an obvious exercise/CBT program versus control, and the methods do not report any blinding of participants or personnel; participants received either structured group training/cognitive-behavioural components or no such program. Comment: Because outcomes included self-reported fear of falling, activity avoidance, quality of life, and falls diary reporting, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the outcomes, so the dominant risk is high.

Summary: The study reports that falls were recorded by the participants in a daily fall diary and monthly calendar, with follow-up telephone interviews after a fall, and that questionnaires were used for fear of falling and quality of life. It also reports objective performance tests (e.g., TUG, chair stand, Romberg/SPPB), but it does not state that outcome assessors were blinded to allocation. Comment: Because the outcomes are a mix of patient self-report and measured tests, and there is no explicit information that an independent/blinded assessor collected or judged the outcomes, blinding of outcome assessment cannot be determined with confidence.

Quote: "Analyses of the primary endpoint, number of falls per individual within a 12 months follow up period, will be performed on base of the full-analysis set (complete information regarding the primary outcome parameter). Within this population, all subjects are considered regardless to their actual compliance and the exercise-compliance of the responsible physiotherapists respectively (intention to treat principle)." Comment: The study states an ITT/full-analysis-set approach with complete primary outcome information, and it also describes active follow-up procedures for missing fall diaries (phone contact if diaries were not returned). Although the paper does not give detailed per-arm attrition numbers in the provided text, the available description suggests follow-up was handled to minimize missing outcome data and there is no signal of imbalanced or outcome-related dropout sufficient to suggest attrition bias.
